coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots-polyfluorene hybrid nanobiosensor for mitomycin C-DNA interaction sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Emre, Deniz
Denizhan, Nuray
Ozkan-Ariksoysal, Dilsat
Bilici, Ali
Sonkaya, Ömer
Algi, Fatih
Yilmaz, Selehattin
coredata.dc:description: A novel graphene quantum dots (GQD) / polyfluorene (PF) nanocomposite was deposited on the disposable pencil tip graphite electrode (PGE) and proven to be an efficient nanosensor for analysis of the electrochemical interaction between the antitumor compoundmitomycinC(MC) with double stranded DNA (ds-DNA). This modified electrode (GQD@PF-PGE) was prepared in four steps: hydrothermal, chemical oxidation, ultrasonication and electro-oxidation processes. GQD, PF, GQD@PF and GQD@PF-PGE have been characterized by different analytical techniques such as SEM, TEM, XRD, FTIR, UV–Vis, EIS. Compared to bare PGE, GQD@PF modified PGE performed approximately 56 times more sensitive analysis when evaluating the guanine oxidation signals measured by DPV. CV and EIS measurements also showed that GQD@PF-PGE possesses a fast electron transfer as compared to bare electrode and exhibit a remarkable electrocatalytic activity towards both guanine and MC electrooxidation. Comprehensive optimization studies have also been carried out for the developed new nanobiosensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical DNA biosensor
Polyfluorene
Electropolymerization
Mitomycin C
Pencil graphite electrode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: GQD@PF synthesis and electrode modification process
The preparation and characterization of hybrid material, and modified electrode
Modification and characterizations of the disposable electrode surfaces
Experimental optimization based on guanine oxidation signal
Electrochemical oxidation of mitomycin C at PGE or modified electrodes
The influence of pH on the peak height and peak potential of mitomycin C
Voltammetric determination of DNA interaction with MC using GQD@PF-modified electrode

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Canakkale Onsekiz Mart University Scientific Research Commission

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank Canakkale Onsekiz Mart University Scientific Research Commission ( FHD-2021-3698 ) for the financial support. Authors thank Dr. Alper Fitoz and Prof. Zehra Yazan for impedance measurements, and Onko Kocsel Pharmaceuticals for supplying MC.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: General synthesis procedures for GQD [20], PF [21], GQD@PF.
FTIR spectra of GQD, PF and GQD@PF materials (A), and bare and modified PGEs (B).
SEM images of GQD (a, b), PF (c, d), and GQD@PF (e, f).
Nyquist diagrams of bare, and GQD@PF (10 μg mL−1) modified PGEs (A), CVs of 1 mM K3Fe(CN)6 and 100 mM KCl on PGE (a) and GQD@PF (b) at 100 mV s−1 scan rate (B).
The cyclic voltammograms of a solution containing 1 mM K3Fe(CN)6 and 100 mM KCl, obtained from PGE (A) and GQD@PF-PGE (B) at 10–1000 mV s−1 scan rates.

DPVs of bare PGE (A), and modified PGE (10 μg mL−1 GQD@PF) (B), and inset calibration plots of guanine signals obtained from DNA concentrations of 100, 200, 300, 400, 500 μg mL−1 for PGE (A), and 40, 60, 80, 100 μg mL−1 for modified PGE (B). The histogram (C) shows the guanine signals of 100 μg mL−1 DNA at bare and modified PGEs at optimum conditions.

DP Voltammograms of 100 μg mL−1 DNA (a), 100 μg mL−1 DNA + 100 μg mL−1 MC (b) and 100 μg mL−1 MC (c) on bare PGE (A) and modified PGE (B), and related histogram of peak currents (C). Measurements were conducted in 0.5 M ABS (pH 4.75) buffer (mean ± standard deviation of 5 measurements).
Differences of anodic and cathodic peak potentials relative to each other (ΔEp[a-c] ) of the redox reaction in 1x10-3 M K3Fe(CN)6 and 0.1 M KCl at PGE and GQD@PF-PGE (100 mV s−1, scan rate).

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots-polyfluorene hybrid nanobiosensor for mitomycin C-DNA interaction sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Aksaray University, Department of Molecular Biology & Genetics & ASUBTAM M. Bilmez BioNanoTech Lab, Aksaray, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel graphene quantum dots (GQD) / polyfluorene (PF) nanocomposite was deposited on the disposable pencil tip graphite electrode (PGE) and proven to be an efficient nanosensor for analysis of the electrochemical interaction between the antitumor compoundmitomycinC(MC) with double stranded DNA (ds-DNA). This modified electrode (GQD@PF-PGE) was prepared in four steps: hydrothermal, chemical oxidation, ultrasonication and electro-oxidation processes. GQD, PF, GQD@PF and GQD@PF-PGE have been characterized by different analytical techniques such as SEM, TEM, XRD, FTIR, UV–Vis, EIS. Compared to bare PGE, GQD@PF modified PGE performed approximately 56 times more sensitive analysis when evaluating the guanine oxidation signals measured by DPV. CV and EIS measurements also showed that GQD@PF-PGE possesses a fast electron transfer as compared to bare electrode and exhibit a remarkable electrocatalytic activity towards both guanine and MC electrooxidation. Comprehensive optimization studies have also been carried out for the developed new nanobiosensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mitomycin C, which was discovered as an antibiotic in 1956 in Japan, is currently used as an antitumor agent [1]. This compound is isolated from the bacterium Streptomyces caespitosus. MC is an alkylating reagent which blocks DNA synthesis and inhibits cell mitosis. Therefore, it is used in chemotherapy of non-small cell lung tumors, bladder, prostate and other types of cancer [2]. The oxidized form of MC is inactive in DNA synthesis inhibition [3]. The activation is achieved by chemical or enzymatic reduction. The reduced MC binds to DNA from the N2– position of guanine via alkylation and leads to inter-strand DNA crosslinks [3]. The cross-linking between the two helixes prevents the unwinding of the double helix structure of DNA during mitosis phases and thus prevents mitosis [3]. Interactions between DNA and drugs have been investigated by various techniques such as UV–Vis., FT-IR, Raman, NMR spectroscopies, mass spectrometry, electrophoresis, atomic force microscopy (AFM) [4]. Electrochemical DNA biosensors are also widely used to investigate these interactions due to their advantages such as cheapness, convenience, speed, low detection limit, and wide working range [5]. Modification of electrode surfaces with nanomaterials offers advantages such as enhanced surface area, biocompatibility, efficient mass transport to the surface, etc [5]. More sensitive and more selective electrochemical sensors are achieved by development of innovative nanomaterials. For this purpose, carbon-based nanotechnology products such as graphene oxide types (GO, rGO), carbon nanotubes (CNT), quantum dots (QDs) and their derivatives have been commonly used for sensor applications [6]. Among such nanomaterials, classical semiconductor QDs, recently produced graphene quantum dots (GQD), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been studied comprehensively up to now [7]. In fact, since their discovery in 1983, the rate at which QDs have been used for the benefit of society has occurred at an unprecedented rate compared to other nanomaterials. In the literature, there are some prominent studies on various QDs and their electrical properties, their use in electrochemical sensors for the determination of protein, DNA, etc. biomolecules, and the future of QDs [8,9].

On the other hand, due to their outstanding physical and electrical properties, GQD, which is the latest member of the graphenes, were recently exploited as promising carbon nanomaterials in electrochemical sensing applications [8,10].

GQDs are nanometer-sized graphene pieces (1–50 nm) with unique properties. Some of these are size-dependent photoluminescent, large surface area, electro-catalytic features, photostability, biocompatibility, and low toxicity, edge effects [11]. They have multiple functional groups on their structure such as hydroxyl, carboxyl [11]. Thanks to these active sites, they can easily interact with a variety of biomolecules and conjugated polymers [11,12]. GQDs increase conductivity, electron mobility and transport of the target molecule on electrode. Therefore, it is accepted that enhanced electrochemical response is obtained [11]. In addition, their size, structure, and properties can be regulated by different synthesis conditions or functionalization of their surfaces [12]. For surface functionalization, doping with heteroatoms and preparation of their nanocomposites with CPs are preferred strategies [12].

Recently, sensing performance of electrodes were enchanced by modifying their surfaces with GQDs and conductive polymer (CP) binary systems, such as GQD/Polyaniline (PANI), GQD/Polypyrrole (Ppy) ve GQD/Polyindole (PIn). For example, GQD/Ppy composite was prepared to modify the GCE surface, and the designed nanomaterial contained sensor was used for the voltammetric analysis of bisphenol A in real samples (water) with high sensitivity and selectivity [13]. GQD/PIn-based electrochemical sensors was prepared for dopamine detection [14]. Cai et. al. studied voltammetric determination of calycosin on GQD/PANI modified GCE [15].

Polyfluorenes (PFs) are a member of conductive polymer family [16]. Due to their photoactive and electroactive properties, PFs were exploited in chemicals sensor, light emitting diodes and photovoltaic applications. PFs have high surface area, porosity and unique thermochromic and solvatochromic properties [17]. Modifications on the C-9 carbon expand their application areas. For example, such a modification process allows for the preparation of various specific sensors [16]. However, PFs are seldom exploited as electrochemical sensing materials in comparison with other CPs.

In the current study, a new nanocomposite, GQD@PF, based on GQDs and PF was synthesized and used for the first time as a novel nanobiosensor platform for the enhanced monitoring of the interaction between dsDNA and MC by modifying the single-use pencil tip graphite electrode (PGE) surfaces with this composite. The properties of GQD@PF modified PGE system were evaluated comprehensively by using both unmodified PGEs and the nanocomposite-modified ones to clarify the signal increase due to the presence of GQD@PF at the sensing part of the nanobiosensor. GQD@PF modified PGE surfaces were characterized by using SEM, TEM, UV–Vis, FTIR, XRD, EIS and CV methodologies. Individual effects of DNA and MC concentrations on the electrochemical response and MC-DNA interaction time were also examined to determine optimum sensor performance, and the findings were discussed in the following sections along with future prospects.
This section was presented in Supplementary note 1.
Details on the microscopic, spectroscopic and electrochemical characterization techniques performed in this study are presented in Supplementary note 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD@PF synthesis and electrode modification process
ce:section.ce:para: Modification process was conducted as described in the reference [18], and details were given in Supplementary note 3.
These studies were performed according to the literature [19], and details were given in Supplementary note 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation and characterization of hybrid material, and modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
GQD was synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid [20]. PF was obtained by oxidatition reaction of AF with NH4(S2O8)2 [21]. GQD@PF was prepared by ultrasonication of GQD with PF solutions. The general synthetic procedure is outlined in Fig. 1
[20,21]. Their characterizations were conducted by UV, Vis, FTIR, XRD, SEM, TEM and CV analysis.

The UV–vis spectra of GQD, PF, GQD@PF are given in Fig. S1. AF (monomer) has an absorption maximum at 300 nm (not given here) [22]. However, after polymerization reaction, 80 nm redshift was observed in absorption maximum. This is an evidence of increased conjugation length and polymer formation. Similar results have also been reported for other AF polymers synthesized and compatible with the literature [21–23].

GQD is characterized by two absorption bands centered at 285 and 382 nm. The first absorption band is due to the π-π transitions of the aromatic sp2 structures of GQD. The other absorption band at 382 nm is attributed to the n-π transitions of the functional groups in the GQD structure [24]. A blue shifted absorption band was observed after hybridization process. This assigns that there is a physical interaction between both components (caused by hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions) and a successful nanocomposite synthesis has been performed [25,26].
FTIR spectra of GQD, PF, GQD@PF materials (A), and bare / modified PGEs (B) are given in Fig. 2 .

For GQD, the main peaks at 2992 cm−1 (–OH, –COOH vibrations), 1550 cm−1 (-C=O vibrations), 1380 cm−1 (–COO vibrations), 1170 cm−1 (-C-O vibrations) were observed [27]. For PF, a broad band observed between 3000 and 3700 cm−1 due to hydrogen bonds between polymer chains. The other peaks were observed at 1604 cm−1 (-C=N), 1455 cm−1 (-C=C), 1313 and 1246 cm−1 (C-N benzenoid vibrations), and 1109 cm−1 (C-N quinonoid vibrations). In addition, the peak at 730 cm−1 assigns fluorene couplings via C–C bonds and indicates that the successful polymerization reaction was conducted [21–23].

After hybridization, hydrogen bonds belong to each component were weaker and wider. Similar observations were also reported by others [24,27]. Differently from characteristic GQD and PF peaks, some vibrations were also recorded at 1578 cm−1 (-C=N and -C=C), 1455 cm−1 (-C=C) and 1243 cm−1 (-C-N). After the hybridization, the intensity of 1578 cm−1 peak was increased.

Fig. S2 shows XRD spectra of GQD, PF and GQD@PF. GQD is characterized by two major diffraction peaks observed 2θ values at 11.3° and 17.9°. These sharp peaks indicate that GQDs are in the sodium salts forms [28]. For PF, the diffraction peaks were observed at 2θ values ​​at 11.0, 18.1, 20.5, 22.1, 22.7, 23.6, and 26.7° which shows the semi-crystalline polymer structure probably due to its low molecular weight [21]. At 2 theta regions from 18° to 27°, there were six diffraction peaks were observed due to π – π stacking distances of the PF chains and reported for other fluorene-based polymer and nanocomposites structures [23,29]. The peak patterns of PF and GQD@PF hybrids were similar. However, after hybridization, the diffraction peak intensities decreased, and their positions changed. GQD probably disrupted the crystal structure of PF, resulting in the appearance of broadened peak patterns of GQD/PF. In addition, the decreasing peak intensities of GQD/PF also indicated decreased crystallinity in hybrid structure.
Fig. 3 shows SEM images of GQD, PF, GQD@PF.

In Fig. 3a and 3b, characteristic spherical quantum dots formations were realized. These images showed successful preparation of GQDs. Fig. 3c and 3d show the heterogeneous, tightly packed structure of the PF. Fig. 3e and 3f images show that the GQD particle are embedded in the PF surfaces.

Fig. S3 shows TEM images of GQD, PF, GQD@PF. The sphere-like formations were observed in Fig. S3a and S3b. However, clustered dots are also noticed in some regions (as given in Fig. S3b). Fig. S3c and S3d show the structure of PF consisting of heterogeneous, clustered particles. TEM images of the GQD@PF hybrid in Fig. S3e and S3f show that the hybrid material is also consisted of heterogeneous, clustered nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification and characterizations of the disposable electrode surfaces
ce:section.ce:para: For the electrochemical deposition process of GQD@PF on the PGE surface was obtained by applying five consecutive CVs at potential range from −1.5 V to + 2.5 V with the 100 mV s−1 of scan rate of (Fig. S4). The modified electrode surface was characterized by EIS, CV, and FTIR measurements. Fig. 2B shows FTIR spectra of modified, and unmodified electrodes. In these figures, FTIR spectrum of unmodified electrode is presented to monitoring the changes after modification. As given in previous work, the following PGE signals were obtained at 2952, 1259,1086, 1018, and 796 cm−1 [30]. After modification, there was a slight broadening in the peaks. Differently from PGE signals, the peaks belong to hybrid material were observed between 1300 and 3600 cm−1 and 800–730 cm−1 in modified electrode surface. FTIR spectrum of modified electrode surface exhibited the peaks at 1397 cm−1 (–COOH vibrations for GQDs), 1455 cm−1 (C=C stretching vibrations for PF,) 764 and 731 cm−1 (C–C coupling vibrations for PF.) This spectrum that belongs to modified PGE clearly shows the presence of characteristic signals of both components on the electrode surface.
Impedimetric and voltammetric measurements of GQD@PF-PGE are presented in Fig. 4 and these results are compared with the bare electrode.

When the semicircle diameters, which are the basic criteria in the determination, are examined, it is seen in the Nyquist impedance spectrum graph that it is wide in the bare electrode and quite narrow in the GQD@PF modified electrode (Fig. 4A). While the average Rct of unmodified electrode was obtained to be 280 ± 8.13 Ohm (RSD: 2.90 %, n = 3), after GQD@PF modification onto the electrode surface, it was observed that the Rct value was 39.1 ± 1.67 Ohm (RSD: 4.27 %, n = 3). As a result, the Rct signal after electrode modification showed a dramatic decrease of 86 % compared to before modification. The low surface resistance of GQD@PF-PGE assigned the successful modification process was performed. In addition, this difference between the responses obtained from bare and modified PGEs can be explained by both the increase in the surface area and the greater electron transfer (high conductivity) in the Nernst diffusion layer on the electrode surface after modification.

CV technique was used to electrochemical characterization of bare/modified PGEs and investigate the effect of GQD@PF modification on the responses obtained from electrode surfaces. In this experiment, measurements using both electrodes were made in a solution phase that containing 1 mM K3Fe(CN)6 and 100 mM KCl by CV (Fig. 4B). Changes at the anodic peak current (Ia) was evaluated before and after modification. According to the Fig. 4B-a, the average peak current of bare electrode was obtained as 21 ± 1 μA with the relative standard deviation (n = 3, RSD 4.7 %), (Fig. 4B-a). A very significant increase in Ia signal was obtained with the modified electrode at 10 µg mL−1 concentration level of GQD. The average signal was observed for modified PGE as 363 ± 18 μA (n = 3, RSD 4.4 %), (Fig. 4B-b). The data obtained show that the GQD modification expands the electroactive surface area to a grate extent and there is an increase in electrochemical signals both through this expansion and also because this material also increases conductivity.
The difference in ΔEp[a-c] value between PGE and GQD@PF-PGE showed that the electroactivity and surface properties of the electrode changed after the modification process [31].

ESA, defined as electroactive surface area, was calculated separately for unmodified and GQD modified PGEs using the Randles-Ševčík equation using Fig. 5
[32]. The ESAs of the PGE and GQD@PF-PGE was found as 0.025 (±0.001) cm2 and 0.608 (±0.008) cm2, respectively. An approximately 24 fold increase in ESA value was observed when the electrode surface was modified with GQD@PF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental optimization based on guanine oxidation signal
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of GQD@PF concentration on the DNA signal was also studied at several concentrations of GQD@PF from 0 to 40 µg mL−1 (data not shown). According to the results the optimum concentration for GQD@PF modification onto the electrode was defined as 10 µg mL−1.

The effect of GQD@PF sonication time on the guanine response was also investigated. For this purpose, 1000 µg mL−1 GQD@PF was sonicated for 5, 10 and 20 min and the diluted suspension of this nanomaterial modified onto PGE surface by CV technique. Then, 100 µg mL−1 DNA was immobilized to the nanomaterial coated surface and the measurement was taken with DPV which was given in Fig. S5. Since the best signal was obtained with 10 µg mL−1 GQD@PF prepared in 0.05 M PBS (pH 7.4) after 10 min sonication from 1000 µg mL−1 master stock, the GQD@PF concentration to be modified was 10 µg mL−1 (Fig. S5). In subsequent experiments, 10 µg mL−1 concentration level of GQD@PF was used for modification.
In the experiment of detecting the optimal DNA amount for the GQD@PF-PGE, the concentration range of dsDNA was preferred from 10 to 500 µg mL−1 (Fig. 6 ).

According to the results, a gradual increase in signal is observed from 20 to 500 µg mL−1 of concentration values and it is understood that the developed nanomaterial adsorbs a very large amount of DNA to its surface. However, we have evaluated that the peak height of DNA at a concentration of 100 µg mL−1 is sufficient enough for high amount of DNA coverage at the surface of the GQD@PF-PGE. Thus, that value was chosen as the optimum dsDNA concentration for further experiments. The histogram in Fig. 6C shows the signal increase under optimum conditions. The average guanine signal for 100 µg mL−1 DNA was 0.7 ± 0.1 μA (RSD: 8.9 %, n = 3) as studied with bare PGE, and 39.3 ± 0.2 μA (RSD: 0.53 %, n = 3) after modification of PGE with 10 µg mL−1 GQD@PF (Fig. 6). As a result, with the effect of nanomaterial modification on the sensor surface and thus enriched surface area, the DNA signal increased remarkably about 56 times.

According to the IUPAC definition [33] and literature [34] the the detection limit (LOD) was calculated as 0.545 ng and 0.039 ng for bare and modified PGEs, respectively in 50 μL of sample volume with the aid of the linear part of the calibration curve in Fig. 6A and 6B based on Eq. (1):
(1)
LOD = 3
S b
/ q
where Sb is the standard deviation of blank (in this work we used the SD of the response related to the supporting electrolyte in the absence of DNA), q is the slope of the calibration graph (Fig. 6A and 6B, insets).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical oxidation of mitomycin C at PGE or modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Voltammograms of the oxidation of 50 µg mL−1 MC obtained by CV technique on PGE and GQD@PF-PGE are given in Fig.S6. In Figure S7, the electrochemical behavior of the drug is evaluated collectively. CVs of MC at bare, and modified PGEs (Fig. S6A), CVs at various scan rates (Fig. S6B), and DPV peak current histogram of bare, and modified PGEs (Fig. S6C) were presented respectively. On the PGE surface, an indistinct voltammogram of the oxidation of MC was obtained at very low current. However, a distinct voltammogram was obtained using GQD@PF-PGE, and a higher current at 1.0 V was observed with GQD@PF-PGE compared to bare PGE. (Fig. S7A).

The histograms of the peak currents of MC obtained from bare PGE in 0.067 M phosphate (pH 7.4) buffer, and GQD@PF-PGE in 0.5 M acetate (pH 5.5) buffer using DPV technique were given in Fig. S6C. The peak current values for MC were 0.1 µA for PGE, and 2.7 µA for GQD@PF-PGE. The peak current of MC increased by 27 times after modification of PGE with GQD@PF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of pH on the peak height and peak potential of mitomycin C
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the peak potential and magnitude of MC signal was investigated in GQD@PF-PGE electrodes. For this purpose, electrochemical measurements were taken using DPV technique on modified electrodes in ABS (pH 3.0–5.0), 0.04 M BR buffer (pH 2.01–12.00) and 0.067 M PBS (pH 5.0–8.0). For GQD@PF-PGE, 0.5 M acetate (pH 5.5) buffer solution with the highest current was selected. Subsequent studies were carried out using this pH and supporting electrolyte. These measurements made with the DPV technique are given in Fig. S7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Voltammetric determination of DNA interaction with MC using GQD@PF-modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, it was aimed to investigate the interaction between DNA and MC by using newly-developed GQD@PF nanomaterial modified electrode. Here, MC was used as an antitumor agent that interacts with dsDNA [35] and it is also aimed to measure the capacity of the newly-developed GQD-based nanobiosensor in compound-DNA interaction analysis. To this end, a series of experiments were made to find the optimal interaction conditions related to MC and DNA that were presented above. In the study, to analyze the DNA-MC interaction electrochemically, both the guanine peak region and the MC peak region were examined because both the guanine base and the MC drug molecule are electroactive.

Fig. 7 A shows DPVs of MC and guanine peaks obtained from the bare electrode that was modified with 100 μg mL−1 concentration level of dsDNA in the absence of MC (Fig. 7A-a, green line), 100 μg mL−1 concentration level of MC in the absence of dsDNA (Fig. 7A-c, red line) and after the interaction of 100 μg mL−1 dsDNA with 100 μg mL−1 MC at the electrode (Fig. 7A-b, blue line).

According to the Fig. 7A, while there was dsDNA on the sensing surface of the bare PGE, the peak of 0.7µA of guanine was observed at nearly +0.80 V (Fig. 7A-a). When there was just MC on the PGE transducer, a current of 0.1 µA was acquired about +0.92 V (Fig. 7A-c) which belongs to the MC [5]. After the interaction between dsDNA and the antitumor compound MC, a prominent decrease in the peak current belongs to the guanine was observed (Fig. 7A-b). Consequently, an approximately 30 % of diminish in the magnitude of guanine response was recorded after DNA-MC interaction compared to the peak height observed before the interaction. This decline in guanine peak current is also contained in previous literature investigating the same interaction [35,36]. As it is known, MC is a therapeutic agent with cytotoxic character and damages DNA in both healthy and tumor cells. It has been proven in the literature that MC interacts with guanine bases in DNA and disrupts DNA synthesis by cross-linking with them [2,37,38]. This causes inhibition of DNA replication and mitosis, thus avoiding the proliferation of tumor cells. On the other hand, when measurements were taken after the interaction, 98 % of dramatic decrease in the MC signal at about +0.92 V was obtained (Fig. 7A-b). This can be explained by the mono- and bifunctional alkylation between DNA and MC in addition to cross-links occurring in ds DNA structure [39] As a result of this situation, the MC may have become closed to oxidation. Our results also confirm that MC reduces the electrochemical signal of guanine base. Based on this known information, the effect of nanomaterial on MC-DNA interaction determination was examined in this study.

Later, the same experiments as with bare PGE were also performed with the nanomaterial modified electrode and the obtained peak currents related to guanine and MC were given in Fig. 7B. As seen in the figure, after the interaction between DNA and MC, peak currents of guanine and MC decreased approximately 46 %, and 98 %, respectively (Fig. 7B-b) in comparison to the peak observed before the interaction (Fig. 7B-a and Fig. 7B-c).

To summarize, in bare PGE, guanine average signal alone was 0.7 µA before DNA-MC interaction, and 0.5 µA after the interaction (Fig. 7A). However, in GQD@PF-PGE, guanine signals were 39.3 µA, and 20.9 µA, before and after the interaction, respectively (Fig. 7B). Also Fig. 7C presents the histograms of peak currents with error bars prepared from Fig. s7A and 7B.

When the results of all these electrochemical experiments are evaluated, it has been shown that the dsDNA-MC interaction can be detected with the newly developed nanomaterial modified biosensor more sensitively compared to the unmodified biosensor.

ce:list-item.ce:para: In this study, a new GQD hybrid material (GQD@PF) was synthesized and characterized.
GQD@PF nanocomposite was used to modify PGE surface for precise determination of the dsDNA-MC interaction.

Before the interaction analysis, both DNA alone and drug molecule alone were immobilized on the GQD@PF modified electrode surface. In these experiments, the aim was to test whether this modification increases biomolecule/compound binding to the sensor surface. When the obtained results are evaluated, it is seen that the affinity of DNA to the surface of the GQD@PF modified electrode has increased approximately 56 times compared to the bare electrode. Additionally, the binding rate of the MC drug molecule to the modified electrode increased by 27 times.

It was observed that anticancer drug MC interacted with dsDNA and decreased both guanine and MC signals after the interaction. These interactions have been interpreted as DNA damage after drug-DNA binding.
To detect the dsDNA-MC interaction, GQD@PF was successfully used as a modification agent with high sensitivity and good stability.

In bare PGE, approximately 30 % decrease in guanine signal was observed as a result of dsDNA-MC interaction. When the same measurement was made in the modified GQD@PF-PGE, nearly 46 % decrease in guanine signal was observed. Thus, it was concluded that the current responses on the modified GQD@PF-PGE for dsDNA-MC interaction are more sensitive than on the bare PGE.

ce:section.ce:para: Deniz Emre: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Nuray Denizhan: Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Dilsat Ozkan-Ariksoysal: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ali Bilici: Conceptualization, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ömer Sonkaya: Investigation. Fatih Algi: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Selehattin Yilmaz: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Canakkale Onsekiz Mart University Scientific Research Commission (FHD-2021-3698) for the financial support. Authors thank Dr. Alper Fitoz and Prof. Zehra Yazan for impedance measurements, and Onko Kocsel Pharmaceuticals for supplying MC.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2023.116944.

ce:section.ce:para:
